ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general gnocchi food product class; no specific entry for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina with tomato and mozzarella. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers gnocchi as a pasta/dumpling category. No specific Sorrentina variant exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual Italian dish preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gnocchi alla Sorrentina is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of potato gnocchi topped with tomato sauce, melted buffalo mozzarella, and fresh basil, served as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Gnocchi, potato, cooked; Tomato sauce; Mozzarella, whole milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked potato gnocchi (FDC ID 168153), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170461), and whole-milk mozzarella (FDC ID 170845), weighted by approximate recipe proportions: 55% gnocchi, 30% tomato sauce, 15% mozzarella. Energy cross-checked against published Italian food composition data (CREA — Banca Dati di Composizione degli Alimenti per Studi Epidemiologici in Italia).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; potato gnocchi GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina as a composite dish. GI of 68 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for potato gnocchi (GI ~68, medium category), which forms approximately 55% of the dish by weight. The tomato sauce and mozzarella components contribute negligible glycemic load. This is consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 69.3) / 100 ≈ 47.1, rounded to 47.2 for a 350 g serving providing approximately 69.3 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1085 mg sodium per standard 350 g restaurant serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single restaurant serving of Gnocchi alla Sorrentina may contribute approximately 47% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the American Heart Association; portion reduction or requesting lower-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index of 68 and high glycemic load of approximately 47 per restaurant serving due to substantial refined carbohydrate content from potato gnocchi
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size of Gnocchi alla Sorrentina to approximately 150–200 g and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response, given the medium GI and high per-serving glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from buffalo mozzarella contributing approximately 2.6 g per 100 g, combined with moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, Gnocchi alla Sorrentina should be consumed in moderated portions; the saturated fat from buffalo mozzarella and the sodium from tomato sauce and cheese together warrant attention in the context of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gnocchi alla Sorrentina is a classic baked Italian pasta dish originating from Sorrento in the Campania region of southern Italy. It consists of soft potato dumplings (gnocchi) made from mashed potato, flour, and egg, first boiled until they float and then transferred to a baking dish, covered generously with a slow-cooked San Marzano tomato sauce, torn fresh buffalo mozzarella, grated Parmigiano-Reggiano, and fresh basil leaves, then baked or gratinated until the cheese melts and forms golden bubbles. The texture is pillowy and yielding from the gnocchi, contrasted by the silky, slightly acidic tomato sauce and the creamy, stretchy mozzarella. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and mildly tangy, with the sweetness of ripe tomatoes balancing the milky fat of the buffalo mozzarella. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal providing approximately 142 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the mozzarella and a moderate saturated fat content. The glycaemic index is approximately 68 (medium), and a standard restaurant serving of 350 g carries a high glycaemic load of approximately 47, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is notable at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, relevant for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, contains gluten, milk, and egg allergens, and is not certified halal due to the use of animal rennet in traditional mozzarella production. In Singapore, it is served at Italian restaurants such as Ricciotti at The Riverwalk and is priced at the premium end of the casual dining market.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB, USDA, ICMR-NIN) contains a direct entry for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries for cooked potato gnocchi, tomato sauce, and whole-milk mozzarella, weighted by approximate recipe proportions. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for potato gnocchi. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked potato gnocchi (FDC ID 168153), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170461), and whole-milk mozzarella (FDC ID 170845), weighted at approximately 55:30:15 by mass. Direct laboratory analysis of this dish as served at Ricciotti Singapore is not available. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in the USDA source data for these ingredients — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component USDA source entries for this dish combination and cannot be defensibly estimated. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 5. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for potato gnocchi; no direct GI measurement for Gnocchi alla Sorrentina as a composite dish was found in the approved GI literature. 6. The input food_name 'Gnocchi alla Sorentina' contains a common spelling variant (single 'r'); the canonical Italian spelling 'Gnocchi alla Sorrentina' (double 'r') is used as food_name; the variant spelling is preserved in aliases. 7. Halal status is uncertain; traditional buffalo mozzarella uses animal rennet which may not be halal-certified; is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 9. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (68 × 69.3 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 47.2.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected canonical food_name spelling from 'Gnocchi alla Sorentina' to 'Gnocchi alla Sorrentina'; preserved input variant in aliases.
Built composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (gnocchi, tomato sauce, mozzarella) weighted by approximate recipe proportions.
Sourced GI analogously from University of Sydney GI Database potato gnocchi entry (GI 68); documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Set vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg to -1 with explicit notes documentation per G3 sentinel value rule.
Set trans_fat_g to 0.0 with confirmed zero justification in notes per zero-macro documentation rule.
Verified all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list per aliases consistency rule.
Confirmed gi_category 'medium' is correct for GI 68 per GI threshold rule (56–69 = medium).
Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed strings ('closest_parent', 'none').
Confirmed G6 multilingual compliance: Italian cuisine has no mandatory non-English local name requirement.
